Summary: Added containerization support for the backend NestJS app and the landing Next.js app in separate directories. The backend now has a multi-stage Dockerfile plus a compose file that loads .env and publishes port 3000. The landing app now has a multi-stage Dockerfile plus a compose file that publishes container port 3000 on host port 3001. Both compose files validated successfully with docker compose config.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Used separate Docker and Compose files per app to keep backend and landing container concerns isolated and maintainable.
2. [finished] Chose Node 22 bookworm-slim images for both apps to keep compatibility broad and avoid Alpine-specific friction.
3. [finished] Mapped the landing app to host port 3001 so it can coexist with the backend service on 3000 without conflicts.

Affected files:

The backend now canonicalizes legacy http://philhealth.gov.ph identifiers for lookup and storage compatibility, while the Flutter sync UI sends the new URI. Verified affected backend and frontend tests pass after the change.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Use http://philhealth.gov.ph/fhir/Identifier/philhealth-id as the canonical PhilHealth identifier system across sync request preparation and payload generation.
2. [finished] Preserve backward compatibility by allowing backend lookup and storage to accept legacy http://philhealth.gov.ph values during the transition.

Summary: Verified that the backend receive-results flow already overwrites the stored profile and marks it sync-locked via the Patient bundle payload, then fixed the Flutter side to refresh authoritative profile data from the backend so synced patient information replaces the cached personal information view. The personal information screen now refreshes on open, and the sync simulation flow refreshes after success; tests confirm the screen shows the synced name and lock state.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep receive-results vs receive-push semantics unchanged; the overwrite behavior belongs to profile refresh state in the app, not to changing the receive-results payload contract.
2. [finished] Use the backend profile endpoint as the source of truth for post-sync UI state so the cached AuthSession profile is replaced instead of merged locally.
3. [finished] Add optional refresh callback hooks to the Flutter screens to make the refresh path testable without network access while keeping production defaults on AuthSession.refreshProfileFromBackend.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.ts — Confirmed the backend receive-results handler already overwrites the stored profile and marks synced patient data as locked. | Notes: No backend code change was required.
2. [inspected] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Confirmed the Patient mapper sets syncLocked: true in the profile patch used by receive-results processing. | Notes: Supports the conclusion that the backend already enforces sync lock state.
3. [modified] frontend/lib/features/auth/domain/auth_session.dart — Added authoritative profile refresh support so the app can overwrite cached profile state with backend data. | Notes: Refresh helper fetches /profile/me and persists the returned profile.
4. [modified] frontend/lib/features/profile/presentation/screens/personal_information_screen.dart — Triggered a profile refresh when the personal information screen opens so synced data replaces stale local values and lock state updates. | Notes: Added optional refresh hook for testability.

[modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/presentation/screens/sync_records_wizard_screen.dart — Refreshed the profile after successful sync simulation before returning to the dashboard so the session cache reflects the synced patient data. | Notes: Added optional refresh hook for testability.

Summary: Fixed the backend webhook routing mismatch that caused /api/v1/fhir/receive-results to 404. The global prefix in backend/src/main.ts now applies cleanly to all routes without excluding the FHIR webhook endpoints, the webhook controller remains mounted at @Controller('fhir'), and the e2e route test now boots with the same api/v1 global prefix used in production. Verification passed for both build and FHIR e2e routing.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Remove the special-case prefix exclusions in main.ts rather than altering the controller path again, because the production API contract should consistently live under /api/v1 and webhook routes should not be special-cased at the prefix layer.
2. [finished] Keep the webhook controller base path as fhir so the final mounted URL remains concise and predictable under the global prefix.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/main.ts — Removed the FHIR-specific prefix exclusions so the global api/v1 prefix applies to the webhook routes. | Notes: This is the production routing fix.
2. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.controller.ts — Kept the webhook controller mounted under fhir so it combines with the global prefix to form /api/v1/fhir/... | Notes: This file was previously corrected away from api/fhir.

Summary: Relaxed personal information save behavior so the frontend and backend no longer require all fields for profile updates, while sync remains gated strictly by identifier presence. The backend now accepts partial profile drafts, merges them with existing profile data, and keeps the sync-lock enforcement introduced earlier. Frontend form fields were changed to optional validators and the sync button still depends on having at least one identifier. Regression tests were updated and targeted Flutter/Jest suites passed.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Preserved the sync-lock behavior from the earlier sync hydration work, but decoupled it from normal save validation so users can save partial profile data before sync completion.
2. [finished] Used backend merge logic as the source of truth for partial profile updates so the frontend can stay simple and avoid duplicating field-preservation rules.
3. [finished] Kept sync availability strictly based on identifier presence instead of overall profile completeness, per the user's request.

Updated regression tests for profile readiness and sync navigation behavior, and validated with targeted Flutter and backend Jest suites.

Decisions:

2. [finished] Kept sync hydration authoritative on backend webhook/resource-processing logic and only reflected lock/readiness behavior in Flutter UI to preserve clean responsibility boundaries.
3. [finished] Preserved existing auth-based simulate flow and dashboard return behavior while expanding prepare flow to initiate real gateway request.

content:
Summary: Fixed the sync simulation failure that surfaced as 'unable to store observation record' by making the backend observation/lab record filter text length-safe before insert. The root cause was an overlong laboratory_result_records.filter_value exceeding the database's 80-character check constraint. Added a reusable filter builder in the FHIR sync mapper so clinical record filter values are bounded, and added regression coverage for the PH Core parsers to assert filterValue length stays within 80 characters. Verified with targeted backend tests, backend build, and the sync simulation e2e route test.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep the fix in the backend mapper rather than in the frontend, because the failure was caused by a database constraint violation on stored observation data.
2. [finished] Use a shared length-safe filter builder for clinical records so the sync flow is protected against future overlong filter values, not just the current observation fixture.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.mapper.ts — Added the length-safe filter builder and updated clinical record mapping so inserted filter values cannot exceed the database constraint. | Notes: This is the primary production fix for the observation storage failure.
2. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/parsers/fhir-parser.registry.spec.ts — Added regression coverage asserting clinical record filter values stay within the 80-character database limit. | Notes: Covers the observation fixture path that previously failed.
3. [inspected] backend/package.json — Confirmed the available backend test and build scripts before running validation. | Notes: No file changes were needed.
4. [inspected] sqls/007_health_records_setup.sql — Confirmed the laboratory_result_records schema and its 80-character filter_value constraint to identify the root cause. | Notes: Read-only inspection only.
5. [inspected] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.ts — Verified the persistence flow that inserts parsed clinical resources into the repository. | Notes: No code change required in this file.

Summary: Fixed the sync simulation auth-context problem by switching the frontend to send the authenticated Supabase access token as a Bearer Authorization header, and by making the backend simulation route resolve the current user from that token through AuthSupportService, with x-user-id only as a fallback. The request body no longer carries profileId, and the simulation still persists the PH Core bundle against the authenticated account via the existing receive-results-for-profile path. Validation passed after the change.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Chose the bearer-token path because the frontend already maintains AuthSession.accessToken and the backend already supports resolving authenticated Supabase users from access tokens.
2. [finished] Kept the profile UUID internal and avoided reintroducing a public profileId field, which keeps the request shape simpler and closer to the WAH4PC workflow.
3. [finished] Retained the x-user-id fallback only as a compatibility path, but made the bearer token the primary source of account context for simulation.

Affected files:
1. [inspected] backend/src/auth/auth.module.ts — Confirmed AuthSupportService is exported so the integration module can reuse existing Supabase-user resolution. | Notes: No code change required here.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.module.ts — Imported AuthModule so the simulation service can resolve the authenticated user from the access token. | Notes: Keeps auth resolution reusable rather than duplicating it.
3. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.controller.ts — Changed the simulation route to accept Authorization and optional x-user-id headers and pass them to the simulation service. | Notes: The route now supports bearer-token auth context.
4. [modified] backend/src/integration/sync-simulation.service.ts — Resolved profile ID from the bearer token using AuthSupportService, with x-user-id fallback, before persisting the simulated PH Core bundle. | Notes: This is the core runtime fix for the missing account context error.
5. [modified] backend/src/integration/dto/simulate-sync-request.dto.ts — Removed profileId from the public simulation DTO so the body only contains request data relevant to the sync action. | Notes: Prevents users from having to provide internal UUIDs.
6. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.types.ts — Removed the obsolete public simulation payload type that exposed profileId. | Notes: Keeps the contract aligned with the new auth-based flow.
7. [modified] backend/test/app.e2e-spec.ts — Updated the simulation e2e test to use a bearer Authorization header and mocked AuthSupportService account resolution. | Notes: Verifies the real auth-context path instead of the fallback header.

Summary: Adjusted the sync simulation flow so the user-facing request no longer carries a profileId in the body. The frontend now sends the authenticated account ID in an internal x-user-id header, while the backend simulation endpoint resolves the profile context from that header and persists the generated PH Core bundle through the existing receive-results-for-profile path. This preserves UUID-based internal storage without exposing profileId to the user and resolved the validation error the user was seeing.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept UUID-based profile IDs internally because the Supabase profiles table uses the auth user UUID as the primary key and related records reference that same key.
2. [finished] Chose an internal header for account context instead of a request-body field so the UI remains simpler and the UUID is not user-facing.
3. [finished] Preserved the existing identifier-based preparation flow and only changed the simulation transport, keeping the broader interoperability behavior stable.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/integration/dto/simulate-sync-request.dto.ts — Removed profileId from the public simulation request DTO so the request body no longer requires a UUID field from the user. | Notes: This is the main API-shape fix.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.controller.ts — Changed the simulation route to read the internal x-user-id header and pass it to the simulation service. | Notes: Keeps account context internal to the transport layer.
3. [modified] backend/src/integration/sync-simulation.service.ts — Updated the simulation service to require a header-derived user ID and persist the simulated bundle against that profile context. | Notes: No longer depends on a body-level profileId.

[modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/data/interoperability_api_client.dart — Changed the simulation client to send x-user-id as a header instead of profileId in the body, and added header merging support to the POST helper. | Notes: The body now matches the user-facing request shape.
5. [modified] frontend/lib/features/interoperability/presentation/screens/sync_records_wizard_screen.dart — Updated the wizard to pass the signed-in session user ID into the simulation client and keep the sign-in guard for missing context. | Notes: The button stays hidden from UUID details.
6. [modified] backend/test/app.e2e-spec.ts — Updated the e2e simulation request to send x-user-id and removed the profileId body field. | Notes: Verifies the new transport shape.
7. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.types.ts — Removed the obsolete simulation payload type that exposed profileId in the public contract. | Notes: Prevents stale typing from reintroducing the field.

content:
Summary: Implemented a new interoperability simulation flow so the Flutter sync wizard can post real PH Core example data into the backend and store it against the signed-in account/profile. The backend now exposes a simulated sync endpoint that reuses the existing prepare flow for validation, loads checked-in PH Core fixtures, and forwards a Bundle into the existing FHIR receive-results persistence path. The frontend gained a neutral secondary Simulate request button under Prepare request, plus request/result plumbing and updated review copy. Validation passed with backend e2e coverage and Flutter analysis.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept simulation orchestration separate from persistence by adding a dedicated backend SyncSimulationService that builds the fixture bundle and delegates actual storage to the existing FhirSyncService.
2. [finished] Reused the existing provider/identifier preparation flow to validate the simulation request before loading fixtures, so the simulation button follows the same account selection rules as the real sync request.
3. [finished] Used a neutral outlined Flutter action button and neutral snackbar styling for the new simulation UX instead of adding a new purple accent treatment.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.module.ts — Exported FhirSyncService so the integration simulation service can reuse the existing persistence flow without duplicating storage logic. | Notes: Module export added for cross-module injection.
2. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.controller.ts — Added the new /interoperability/sync/simulate endpoint alongside the existing prepare route. | Notes: Controller now routes simulation requests to the new service.
3. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.module.ts — Imported FhirSyncModule and registered the new SyncSimulationService provider for the simulation workflow. | Notes: Keeps integration orchestration separate from fixture loading.
4. [modified] backend/src/integration/integration.types.ts — Added an explicit response contract for simulated sync results. | Notes: Frontend can parse the response without loose typing.

Summary: Implemented a strict PH Core-aware FHIR inbound parsing layer for the backend webhook flow. The service now validates inbound receive-results and receive-push payloads through per-resource parser modules before mapping and persisting data, and the shared mapper was improved to handle array-shaped encounter code fields from PH Core examples. Added fixture-driven parser and service tests, and verified the backend e2e suite still passes.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Kept parsing/validation separate from persistence so the webhook service stays thin and parser behavior remains fixture-testable.
2. [finished] Used strict PH Core profile suffix matching instead of loose payload acceptance for supported resource families.
3. [finished] Kept the existing Supabase repository write methods and fed them normalized parser output rather than rewriting persistence logic.
4. [finished] Updated the shared encounter mapping to handle array-shaped FHIR codeable-concept fields because the PH Core encounter fixture uses that shape.

Affected files:
1. [modified] backend/src/fhir-sync/fhir-sync.service.ts — Wired inbound webhook handling through the new parser registry before persistence, keeping route handling thin and validating PH Core payloads first. | Notes: receiveResults and receivePush now parse resources before repository writes.

Summary: Fixed the PowerShell FHIR seed script so it no longer fails on duplicate profile primary keys. The seed flow now treats the profile created by the auth trigger as an upsert target and also upserts patient_identifiers, making reruns idempotent after partial attempts. The script still seeds PH Core example bundles into /fhir/receive-results for immunization, consultation history, medical history, and laboratory results.

The sync prepare endpoint now responds with HTTP 202 and the tests (unit, build, and e2e) all pass.

Summary: Implemented the first-pass inbound WAH4PC/FHIR sync flow for wah4patients. The backend now has a dedicated fhir-sync module for provider-facing webhooks and practitioner discovery, routes are excluded from the global /api/v1 prefix where needed, patient identifier matching is backed by a new Supabase table, and inbound resources are normalized into the existing internal clinical tables instead of storing raw payloads. 4. [finished] We used the existing internal health-record and medication-resupply tables as the user-facing storage targets for mapped FHIR data rather than creating a parallel raw payload store.

Summary: Implemented stricter field-type validation for the Personal Information flow. The frontend profile form now rejects digits in text fields and letters in numeric fields through validators, and the backend DTOs enforce the same rules for names, communication language, city/province/country, optional personal detail text fields, phone numbers, postal code, PhilHealth/PhilSys IDs, and emergency contact phone. Address lines remain mixed alphanumeric for practical street-address support. Focused backend DTO tests and the Flutter Personal Information screen test passed, and the Kanban card was moved to done.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Validation was enforced in both frontend and backend so the UI provides immediate feedback while the API remains authoritative.
2. [finished] The team kept address fields mixed alphanumeric instead of text-only because numeric house/unit details are common in real addresses.
3. [finished] The Flutter validation test now calls FormState.validate() directly instead of depending on viewport scrolling, which makes the test more stable and focused on validation behavior.

Summary: Implemented field-format validation for the Personal Information profile flow on both frontend and backend. The frontend profile form now validates text-like fields, phone numbers, postal code, and PhilHealth/PhilSys IDs before save. The backend DTOs now enforce matching rules for names, phone numbers, postal code, and identifier formats while still allowing either PhilHealth or PhilSys to satisfy the identifier requirement. Focused backend and Flutter tests passed, and the Kanban card was moved to done.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Validation was implemented in both layers: frontend for immediate feedback and backend DTOs for authoritative enforcement.
2. [finished] Name fields were constrained to letters and basic punctuation, while address line 1 was left more permissive to preserve practical street address inputs.
3. [finished] PhilHealth and PhilSys identifiers accept their respective dashed or undashed numeric formats, which keeps the rules strict but still user-friendly.

Summary: Implemented Phase 2 of the interoperability rollout across backend and Flutter. The backend now exposes a WAH4PC-backed interoperability proxy for provider discovery and sync-request preparation, with env validation and e2e coverage. The Flutter app now launches a dedicated sync wizard from the Personal Information screen, uses bottom-sheet selection for identifier/provider choice, and reuses the existing profile readiness rules to gate entry. Tests for the new interoperability models, wizard flow, and profile-to-wizard navigation all pass.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep backend gateway communication isolated in a dedicated integration module rather than calling wah4pc directly from Flutter, so provider discovery and request prep stay server-owned.
2. [finished] Keep the sync wizard as a route-based multi-step screen rather than collapsing it into a modal, because the flow needs visible step state and room for provider/error handling.
3. [finished] Use a generic secure-storage mock in flutter_test_config.dart instead of per-test stubbing, so existing and future widget tests can call AuthSession.clear() safely.

Summary: Removed PhilSys ID from the backend profile contract and aligned the frontend shared profile model with the trimmed response shape. The backend now no longer validates, stores, returns, or includes philSysId in auth/profile flows, and a SQL migration was added to strip existing philSysId keys from profiles.patient_profile JSONB rows. The frontend profile model and tests were updated to remove the stale field, while the Personal Information screen continues to rely on PhilHealth ID only. Backend build and targeted frontend Flutter tests passed after the change.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep the profile storage as JSONB but remove the philSysId key from both runtime writes and existing stored data rather than changing the table shape, because the field lives inside patient_profile rather than a dedicated column.
2. [finished] Align the frontend shared profile model with the backend removal so the app contract stays consistent and avoids carrying a dead field.
3. [finished] Treat PhilHealth ID as the sole identifier required for current sync readiness after removing PhilSys from the product flow.

Added tests for the readiness helper and updated the Personal Information screen test to cover the locked state and disabled sync button. The phase 1 Kanban card was moved from backlog to in-progress and then to done after validation.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Keep the gateway sync implementation out of scope for Phase 1 and only prepare the profile/readiness layer now, so the later gateway work can build on a stable profile gate.
2. [finished] Preserve both identifier fields in the data model for backward compatibility and future support, but relax the UI so sync only needs one of them instead of requiring both.
3. [finished] Use a small domain helper for readiness logic instead of embedding the rules directly in the screen, to keep presentation and sync rules separated.

Summary: Created a new expanded planning document max-backlog.md for the PH Core Patient Profile plus WAH4PC Sync Integration. Summary: Reviewed the frontend Health Records screens and the existing Personal Records architecture to plan a future local persistence layer for Health Records. The current Health Records feature is still presentation-driven with hardcoded entry data in the screen files, while Personal Records already has a dedicated typed data/repository pattern. The recommended approach is to introduce a separate local health-records repository and domain model layer so the UI can read from a single source of truth now and later support creation without reworking the screens.

Decisions:
1. [ongoing] Use a separate local Health Records module instead of mixing storage logic into the dashboard screens or the presentation widgets, to keep responsibilities separated and future creation flows easy to add.
2. [ongoing] Prefer a single typed local repository/source-of-truth for all Health Records categories, with screen-specific view models mapped from shared record data rather than duplicating storage logic per screen.

Summary: Added a plain-English atlas for WAH4P so future work can read the project like a map. The repo is best understood in layers: a Flutter client that controls navigation and presentation, a NestJS backend that owns security and business rules, and Supabase/Postgres plus secure local storage for persistence. The most important user journeys now have an explicit flow: splash/startup -> onboarding or login -> dashboard or MPIN unlock -> feature areas like records, appointments, resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. This memory also clarifies which screens are static, which are route-driven, and which backend APIs support each major journey.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Use the plain-English layer model for future reasoning: backend = rules and APIs, frontend = screens and navigation, database/docs = persistence and domain model.
2. [finished] When updating routes or screens, treat the route constants and router as the source of truth and update the screen groups from there instead of relying on older inventory documents.
3. [finished] Keep launch logic centralized in the app shell and startup service so startup routing stays easy to understand and does not leak into individual screens.
4. [finished] Keep backend auth responsibilities split by workflow so each part of the account journey remains testable and easy to extend.

Summary: Saved a deeper project map for WAH4P that focuses on navigation, feature boundaries, and service responsibilities. The repository presents a clear two-tier architecture: a NestJS backend that owns auth/security/business rules and a Flutter frontend that owns display, navigation, and device-scoped state. The frontend route tree now has a precise map from startup through onboarding, auth, dashboard, PHR, EHR-style records, appointments, calendar, notifications, profile, and legal screens. The backend API surface is also clearer: a public health check plus a broad auth/profile domain that covers registration, login, 2FA, MPIN, password reset, and profile hydration/update. This memory layer is intended to function as an app map, not just a summary.

Decisions:
1. [finished] Treat backend code as the authority for security, validation, auth workflows, and persistence, and treat the Flutter client as the presentation/navigation layer that consumes those backend capabilities.
2. [finished] Use the new app map as a navigation reference: startup -> onboarding/login/dashboard/unlock, then the dashboard branches into health records, personal records, appointments, medication resupply, calendar, notifications, profile, and security flows.
3. [finished] Keep the backend auth domain split by workflow rather than merging all auth logic into one service: registration, login, password reset, 2FA/TOTP, MPIN, and profile handling remain separate responsibilities.
4. [finished] Preserve the current startup design: the app should decide the initial route from auth/onboarding/device state before the user reaches the main dashboard shell.
5. [finished] Treat the older screen inventory documents as useful but secondary; if they conflict with the live code, the route builder and app route constants win as the authoritative map.

content:
Summary: Audited the WAH4P repository and saved a clear project snapshot for future work. The repo is split into a NestJS backend, a Flutter frontend, and supporting SQL/docs. The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. metadata.summary: Audited the WAH4P repository and saved a clear project snapshot for future work. The repo is split into a NestJS backend, a Flutter frontend, and supporting SQL/docs. The backend is a secured API with global API-key protection, throttling, strict env validation, Supabase auth/data integration, and Resend email delivery. The frontend is a Flutter mobile app with centralized theming, secure local storage, a startup gate, and MPIN/session lifecycle handling. The docs describe a Supabase/Postgres source of truth for patient data, with frontend screens mostly acting as presentation and interaction layers. I also captured the current testing surface and a couple of documentation mismatches that future work should reconcile.
